title: Lavender shortbread
subtitle: Scottish shortbread with dried culinary lavender
excerpt: Classic Scottish shortbread biscuits scented with a small amount of dried culinary lavender. The lavender is ground briefly to break up the flowers and release the oil, then worked into the dough with the icing sugar. The key is proportion: enough to taste, not enough to taste of soap.
sourceNotes: Scottish shortbread is documented in British baking literature from the eighteenth century. The use of lavender as a culinary flavouring in baked goods follows a long European tradition; the combination with shortbread is a modern British adaptation.

recipe.batchNotes: Double the recipe and bake in batches.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps well in an airtight tin for 7 to 10 days. Flavour develops overnight.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan: 140 degrees C; shortbread bakes slowly to stay pale

glossaryTerms.definition: Culinary lavender is dried from food-safe varieties, most commonly Lavandula angustifolia. Decorative lavender is grown with pesticides not cleared for food use and should not be used in cooking. The quantity in baking must be restrained: lavender's essential oil intensifies during baking, so a recipe that smells pleasantly floral in the raw dough can taste strongly of soap once baked. Start with a small amount and adjust; 1 to 2 teaspoons of dried flowers is usually the upper limit for a standard batch of biscuits.

content.text: Lavender shortbread is a biscuit about restraint.

is used at 1 teaspoon of dried flowers per 225 g of flour. Grind the dried flowers lightly in a pestle and mortar or crush them between your fingers before mixing in; this breaks the flowers open and releases the oil more evenly through the dough. The shortbread should taste floral and slightly fragrant, not medicinal.

Crush the dried lavender lightly and rub it into the icing sugar in a large bowl. Beat the softened butter into the lavender sugar until pale and fluffy, about 2 minutes. Add the salt. Mix in the flour until the dough just comes together; do not overwork it. Shape into a flat disc, wrap, and refrigerate for 30 minutes. Preheat the oven to 160 degrees C (conventional). Line two baking trays with baking paper. Roll the cold dough to about 5 mm thick on a lightly floured surface and cut into rounds or fingers. Arrange on the trays and bake for 16 to 18 minutes until pale golden at the edges but still barely coloured on the surface. Leave on the trays for 5 minutes before transferring to a rack. Shortbread firms considerably as it cools; do not judge doneness by softness from the oven.
